coredata.dc:title: Interfacial carbon quantum dot thin film impacts on morphological and chemical structures of fluorine-doped tin oxide films
coredata.dc:creator: Lee, Ki-Won
Jo, Myeong-Hun
Ahn, Hyo-Jin
coredata.dc:description: Morphology and chemical bonding states, including surface roughness, film density, F-doping, and oxygen vacancy (VO) concentration directly affect the electrical and optical properties of fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) films. In this study, morphology and chemical bonding states of FTO films are engineered simultaneously by introducing a carbon quantum dot (CQD) thin film as an interface layer during ultrasonic spray pyrolysis deposition. The abundant oxygen-containing surface functional groups and large surface free energy of the CQD thin film promoted (200) oriented crystal growth and accelerated nucleation of FTO, resulting in smooth and dense FTO film morphology. The abundant carboxyl surface functional groups on the CQDs generate active F− species that are effectively doped into SnO2. Formic acid, formed by dissociation of the carboxyl group, can serve as a strong reducing agent for extracting oxygen atoms from the SnO2 lattice, thereby increasing the VO concentration. The accelerated F-doping and VO concentration result in the generation of additional free electrons. The effects of the optimized CQD on the morphological and chemical structures of FTO films are demonstrated by their superior electrical (sheet resistance of ∼5.7 Ω/□) and optical properties (visible transmittance of ∼85.9 %) compared with bare FTO. Moreover, CQD/FTO used as transparent conducting electrodes in electrochromic (EC) application exhibited improved EC performances, including fast switching speeds (5.3 s and 2.3 s for bleaching and coloration, respectively) and high coloration efficiency (81.1 cm2/C) compared to that of bare FTO.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Interface engineering
Carbon thin film
Deposition
Transparency
Electrical conductivity
Electrochromic properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of bare FTO and CQD/FTO films
Fabrication of bare FTO//VO and 2CQD/FTO//VO electrodes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning
Korea Ministry of Science and ICT

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Nano & Material Technology Development Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by Ministry of Science and ICT (No. 2021M3H4A3A02086102) and the Nano & Material Technology Development Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by Ministry of Science and ICT (No. 2021M3D1A2047047).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorbance spectrum of CQD aqueous dispersion in wavelength range of 200−700 nm, (b) TEM image of CQD aqueous dispersion, (c) photograph of 1CQD/glass, 2CQD/glass, and 3CQD/glass, and (d) transmittance spectra of 1CQD/glass, 2CQD/glass, and 3CQD/glass in wavelength range of 280−800 nm.
(a) Top-view SEM image of 2CQD/glass and AFM images of (b) 1CQD/glass, (c) 2CQD/glass, and (d) 3CQD/glass.

(a) Schematic representation of mechanism of interfacial CQD thin film simultaneously generating engineering of morphology and chemical bonding states of FTO films during USPD, (b) C1s XPS spectrum of CQDs, and (c) TGA/DTA curves of CQDs in temperature range of 24 °C − 900 °C.
(a) FTIR pattern of CQD coated glass substrate, (b) full, (c) O1s, and (d) N1s XPS spectra of CQD powder.
TGA-GC/MS coupled analysis of CQD powder in a temperature range from 30 to 450 °C.

(a) XRD curves within 10°−90°, (b) enlargement of XRD curves within 24°−42°, (c) I(200)/I(110) of all FTO films, and (d) schematic illustration of electrical and optical properties of bare FTO and 2CQD/FTO.
Top-view SEM images of (a) bare FTO, (b) 1CQD/FTO, (c) 2CQD/FTO, and (d) 3CQD/FTO.
Cross-view SEM and AFM images of (a and c) bare FTO and (b and d) 2CQD/FTO.
(a) Sn3d XPS spectra, (b) O1s XPS spectra, (c) peak area ratio of Sn-F/Sn-Sn and VO/O-Sn for all FTO films; (d) schematic illustration of generation of additional free electrons via F-doping and VO.

Transparent conducting properties of all FTO films: (a) transmittance spectrum in wavelength range of 280−800 nm; (b) optical band gap calculated via relationship between (αhv)2 and photon energy; (c) plots of resistivity, carrier concentration, and carrier mobility; and (d) plots of sheet resistance and visible transmittance.
Comparative FOM plots of bare FTO, 1CQD/FTO, 2CQD/FTO, and 3CQD/FTO.
(a) XRD and (b) FTIR curves of VO//bare FTO and VO//2CQD/FTO, and Cross-view SEM images of (c) bare FTO//VO and (d) 2CQD/FTO//VO.

Electrochromic performances of bare FTO//VO and 2CQD/FTO//VO electrodes: (a) cyclic voltammograms in a potential range from −0.5 to 1.0 V; (b) in-situ transmittance variation at the repeated potential steps of −0.5 and 1.0 V for bleaching and coloration, respectively; (c) enlarged transmittance variation in a range of 100−200 s; and (d) in-situ optical density changes as a function of the applied charge density. The coloration efficiency (CE) obtained from the slopes are indicated on the graphs.
Summary of electrical and optical properties of all FTO films.
Summary of electrochromic performances of bare FTO//VO and 2CQD/FTO//VO electrodes.
Summary of electrochromic performances of vanadium oxide film coated fluorine-doped tin oxide film electrodes.

head.ce:title: Interfacial carbon quantum dot thin film impacts on morphological and chemical structures of fluorine-doped tin oxide films

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Seoul National University of Science and Technology, Seoul 01811, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering
Seoul National University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Seoul National University of Science and Technology, Seoul 01811, Republic of Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: K.W. Lee and M.H. Jo contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Morphology and chemical bonding states, including surface roughness, film density, F-doping, and oxygen vacancy (VO) concentration directly affect the electrical and optical properties of fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) films. In this study, morphology and chemical bonding states of FTO films are engineered simultaneously by introducing a carbon quantum dot (CQD) thin film as an interface layer during ultrasonic spray pyrolysis deposition. The abundant oxygen-containing surface functional groups and large surface free energy of the CQD thin film promoted (200) oriented crystal growth and accelerated nucleation of FTO, resulting in smooth and dense FTO film morphology. The abundant carboxyl surface functional groups on the CQDs generate active F− species that are effectively doped into SnO2. Formic acid, formed by dissociation of the carboxyl group, can serve as a strong reducing agent for extracting oxygen atoms from the SnO2 lattice, thereby increasing the VO concentration. The accelerated F-doping and VO concentration result in the generation of additional free electrons. The effects of the optimized CQD on the morphological and chemical structures of FTO films are demonstrated by their superior electrical (sheet resistance of ∼5.7 Ω/□) and optical properties (visible transmittance of ∼85.9 %) compared with bare FTO. Moreover, CQD/FTO used as transparent conducting electrodes in electrochromic (EC) application exhibited improved EC performances, including fast switching speeds (5.3 s and 2.3 s for bleaching and coloration, respectively) and high coloration efficiency (81.1 cm2/C) compared to that of bare FTO.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the advancement of the electronics industry toward automation and the Internet of Things as well as the increasing demand for both, optoelectronic technology has been widely applied in various energy conversion devices, such as solar cells, light-emitting diodes, photovoltaic cells, and electrochromic devices [1,2]. The utilization of optoelectronic devices, which transduce electricity to optics and vice versa, not only yields a high amount of energy via renewable and eco-friendly methods, but also minimizes unnecessary energy consumption via energy conservation [3]. Optoelectronic devices generally exhibit layered architecture that includes active, semiconducting, and transparent conducting electrodes (TCEs) [4]. To realize a seamless conversion between electricity and optics, TCEs are crucial in modulating the light transmissivity into/out of the devices and circulating rapid electron transfer among the conductive layers in optoelectronic devices. Recently, several research groups have attempted to improve the electrical and optical properties of TCEs to enhance optoelectronic device efficiency; however, most existing studies have mainly focused on the modification of active or semiconducting electrodes.

Currently, indium tin oxide (ITO, Sn-doped In2O3) film on a substrate is the most commercially available TCE owing to its superior electrical (sheet resistance of ∼10 to 15 Ω/□) and optical properties (visible transmittance of ∼87 %) [5]. However, several critical aspects limit the further use of ITO TCEs: (i) The increasing cost and shortage of indium resources and (ii) their unsatisfactory flexibility and chemical stability, which hinder the realization of flexible optoelectronic applications [6]. Hence, fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) has been investigated extensively as a TCE owing to its acceptable electrical and optical properties, abundance of resources, price-competitiveness, and excellent mechanical and chemical stabilities [7]. Ultrasonic spray pyrolysis deposition (USPD) is a well-established method for fabricating high-quality FTO film nanostructures via adjustable and non-vacuum systems, whereas chemical/physical vapor deposition methods require a high-vacuum system. Moreover, USPD allows the properties of FTO to be altered, including its morphology, F-doping and oxygen vacancy (VO) concentration, crystal texture, and co-doping by tuning the precursor solution (additives and solvents or mixtures) and process parameters (deposition temperature and time, flow gas atmosphere, and droplet flow rate) [8–10]. Houaidji et al. investigated the effects of co-doping on the structural and optoelectronic properties of F and Ni co-doped SnO2 using a precursor solution composed of tin chloride hydrate and ammonium fluoride as the F-doping source and nickel chloride as the Ni-doping source. As the Ni co-doping concentration ([Ni2+]/[Sn4+]) increased to 3 at %, crystal structure showed a preferential growth direction toward the (200) plane and the crystallite size decreased to a minimum value of 7.3 nm [11]. During the spray pyrolysis deposition of FTO films, Elsherif et al. discovered the optimum deposition temperature of 440 °C, which resulted in textures oriented along (200) and (110) planes, larger mean grain sizes, and higher F-doping concentrations [12]. The superior crystallinity and larger grain size enhanced the carrier mobility, and the increase in the F-doping concentration generated additional free electrons, thus resulting in a higher carrier concentration. However, the introduction of an interfacial thin film layer that facilitates the simultaneous engineering of the morphological and chemical structures of FTO films during USPD has not been reported to date.

Surface morphology of TCEs play a significant role in supplying electron transfer to active layer, which affects the electrochemical kinetics and reversibility. Rough surface morphology of TCEs produces vacant spaces between the TCE and active layer. These vacant spaces function as scattering centers for fast-rate electron transfer and begins oriented delamination centers of active layer, which degrades electrochemical kinetics and reversibility, respectively. Moreover, surface roughness of TCE expands possible photon scattering centers at the interface, hindering the visible light transmittance of optoelectronic devices. Therefore, to obtain a TCE suitable for the applied active layer, the surface structure such as roughness and chemical states of FTO must be explored.

Herein, we propose an approach that allows the morphological and chemical structures of FTO films to be adjusted simultaneously via the introduction of an interfacial carbon quantum dot (CQD) thin film during USPD. Owing to the effects of the interfacial CQD thin films, the CQD/FTO films show a highly smooth and densified film surface morphology with a preferred (200) orientation. Moreover, the oxygen-containing surface functional groups of the interfacial CQD thin film promote the acceleration of F-doping and increase the VO concentration of the FTO films. Accordingly, the optimized CQD/FTO films feature excellent electrical and optical properties without electrical or optical degradation, which is attributable to the presence of an interfacial carbon layer, compared with that of bare FTO. Furthermore, the optimized CQD/FTO film exhibits significantly improved switching speeds and coloration efficiency compared to that of bare FTO when used as TCE in electrochromic (EC) electrodes.

Three types of CQD films were prepared by ultrasonic spray coating an aqueous CQD solution on a glass substrate (2 × 2 cm2 size, 0.7 mm thick, Corning, Eagle XG Slim) for 30, 90, and 150 s (denoted as 1CQD, 2CQD, and 3CQD, respectively). The CQD aqueous solution was prepared by dissolving the CQD powder (0.007 wt %) in deionized (DI) water and stirring it with a magnetic bar at 200 rpm for 1 h, which yielded a transparent blue solution. To synthesize the CQD powder, citric acid (0.47 M, Aldrich) and urea (0.5 M, Aldrich) were dissolved in 50 ml DI water. After stirring it with a magnetic bar at 200 rpm for 1 h, the solution was transferred into an autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After the hydrothermal reaction, the solution was centrifuged at 6,000 rpm to remove excess reactants at the bottom. The centrifuged yellowish solution was dialyzed using a 6 − 8 kD molecular weight cut off membrane (14.6 mm diameter, Spectra/Por, Standard RC Tubing). The dialyzed solution was dried at 50 °C for 24 h, which yielded CQD powder. During the ultrasonic spray coating of the three types of CQD films, the distance between the spray nozzle and glass substrate, the air pressure, the CQD solution injection rate, and the hot-plate temperature were equally conditioned at 30 cm, 100,000 Pa, 4 g/L, and 100 °C, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of bare FTO and CQD/FTO films
ce:section.ce:para: Three types of CQD/FTO films were fabricated by the USPD of the FTO precursor solution on the 1CQD, 2CQD, and 3CQD films (named 1CQD/FTO, 2CQD/FTO, and 3CQD/FTO, respectively). The bare FTO film was fabricated by USPD of FTO precursor solution on the glass substrate without CQD. All FTO precursor solutions were prepared by dissolving tin chloride pentahydrate (0.68 M, SAMCHUN) and ammonium fluoride (1.2 M, JUNSEI) in DI water with 5 vol % ethanol (SAMCHUN), followed by stirring until the solution became transparent. During the USPD of the FTO precursor solution on the three typed CQD films and glass substrates, the atomizing frequency (1.6 MHz), flow rate of precursor droplets (15 L/min), box furnace temperature (420 °C), and deposition time (23 min) were fixed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of bare FTO//VO and 2CQD/FTO//VO electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the optoelectronic properties of bare FTO and 2CQD/FTO, vanadium oxide (VO) film was identically spin-coated on the bare FTO and 2CQD/FTO. To fabricate the precursor solution for spin-coating, 1 wt % vanadium chloride (Alfa Aesar) was dissolved in ethyl alcohol (Aldrich). After stirring for 3 h, the color of the solution changed to transparent green. The solution was transferred into the hydrothermal reactor and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. After the temperature cools down, the resultant solution was immediately centrifuged at high-speed and 4 wt % of the sediment was dissolved in 2 propanol (Aldrich). The fabricated VO solution was subjected to 20 kHz-homogenizer and dispersed for spin-coating. The VO precursor solution was spread over each FTO film and spin-coated twice at 2000 rpm. The VO-coated bare FTO and VO-coated 2CQD/FTO were heated at 450 °C for 1 h in a box furnace, which result in the bare FTO//VO and 2CQD/FTO//VO electrodes, respectively.

The absorbance of the aqueous CQD dispersion was measured in the range of 200−700 nm using ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy (PerkinElmer Lambda35). The fluorescence characteristics of the aqueous CQD dispersions were observed using an UV flashlight (UV-7 W, 395 nm). High-resolution observations of the CQD particles were conducted using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL). The transmittance of all films was measured in the range of 280−800 nm using UV–vis spectroscopy. The atomic force microscope (AFM, Park NX20) images of CQD thin films and FTO films were illustrated by scanning X × Y ranges of 1 μm × 1 μm and 5 μm × 5 μm, respectively. The chemical bonding states of all the samples were observed via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab, Cu Kα radiation) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Nicolet-iS50). The thermogravimetric behavior of the CQDs was measured from 24 to 900 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min using a thermogravimetric analysis/differential thermal analysis (TGA/DTA) system (Shimadzu, DTG-60H). The TGA-gas chromatography/mass spectrometry (TGA-GC/MS, Netzsch, TG209F1-GCMS) coupled analysis was conducted in a temperature range from 30 to 450 °C in He gas atmosphere. The temperature increase rate was conditioned at 10 °C/min. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) intensity was measured in the range of 10°−90° using an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, D/Max-2500 V, 18 kW Cu Kα X-ray source, 2theta angular range from −30 to 150°). The top-view and cross-view morphologies of all the FTO films were examined via scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi, S-4800, 10 kV operating voltage). The film thickness of FTO and VO films was demonstrated via cross-view SEM (Hitachi, S-4800, 10 kV operating voltage). The electrical properties, including the carrier concentration, carrier mobility, resistivity, and sheet resistance, of all the FTO films were measured using a Hall-effect measurement system (Ecopia-HMS3000).

The EC performances of bare FTO//VO and 2CQD/FTO//VO electrodes were evaluated in a three-electrode system. The three-electrode system consisted of bare FTO//VO or 2CQD/FTO//VO as a working electrode, silver wire as a reference electrode, and platinum wire as a counter electrode in an electrolyte. The electrolyte was fabricated by dissolving 2.5 M lithium perchlorate (Aldrich) in a propylene carbonate. The cyclic voltammograms were measured by inducing circulating potential in a range from −0.5 to 1.0 V with a rate of 20 mV/s. The in-situ transmittance variations were recorded under the repeated potentials of −0.5 and 1.0 V for 60 s, respectively.

Fig. 3 a illustrates a mechanism of the interfacial CQD thin film for the simultaneous engineering of the morphology and chemical bonding states of FTO films during USPD. The mechanism involves (i) a smooth surface morphology with a preferred (200) orientation and (ii) the acceleration of F-doping and VO in the FTO crystal. Prior to the USPD of the FTO films, three types of CQD thin films with different thicknesses were uniformly coated on glass substrates (1CQD/glass, 2CQD/glass, and 3CQD/glass) by controlling the coating time during the ultrasonic spray coating of the CQD aqueous solution. The three resultant CQD/glass substrates were placed in a box-shaped furnace for FTO film deposition. Immediately thereafter, ultrasonically atomized precursor droplets were supplied to the surface of the CQD thin film, which underwent thermal pyrolysis, thus resulting in the formation of FTO films on the CQD/glass substrates. Ultrasonic atomization enables the uniform and continuous flow of fine droplets measuring ∼1 µm or less, which can yield FTO films with high uniformity and coverage. The main precursor droplets were composed of SnCl4 and NH4F, which formed FTO film through sequential solvent evaporation, condensation, crystallization, grain growth, and film growth via the following chemical reaction: SnCl4 + NH4F → F:SnO2 + NH4Cl + HF + HCl [15]. The CQD interfacial thin films generated unique engineering effects on the morphology and chemical bonding states of the FTO films during these USPD. In general, FTO films feature interlocked pyramidal crystallites with a preferred (110) orientation, which is associated with their rough surface morphology. This rough film morphology can induce the scattering and reflection of incident light, thus hindering efficient light transmission. By contrast, a smooth FTO film morphology with a preferred (200) orientation was featured in the presence of an interfacial CQD thin film during USPD because of the following reasons: abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the CQDs surface were thermally pyrolyzed into oxygen molecules, thus improving the oxygen concentration near the CQD thin film as well as suppressing {101} F-face growth and accelerating {111} K-face growth, all of which are associated with the preferred (200) orientation [16]. The (200) oriented crystal growth indicates film growth in the direction parallel to the substrate, which reduced the film roughness. Accordingly, the C1s XPS result showed the presence of abundant oxygen-containing functional groups in the CQDs (Fig. 3b). Four specific peaks were identified at ∼283.09, 284.50, 285.62, and 287.93 eV, which corresponded to C=C, C−C, C−O/C−N, and −COOH, respectively [17]. In particular, the evident −COOH peak ratio (32.2 %) clearly verified the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups in the CQDs, which is consistent with the aqueous dispersibility and fluorescence characteristics presented in Fig. 1a. Moreover, the TGA/DTA results of the CQDs indicated the thermal pyrolysis of oxygen-containing functional groups, which resulted in abundant oxygen gases (Fig. 3c). Initially (24 °C−150 °C), the CQDs indicated a slight weight loss owing to water desorption at the surface. As the temperature increased, a significant weight loss occurred along with an endothermic reaction at 250 °C−480 °C. This endothermic reaction is due to the typical thermal behavior arising from the vigorous thermal decomposition of oxygen-containing functional groups on CQDs [18]. To clearly demonstrate the functional groups on CQD, FTIR was analyzed on the CQD coated glass substrate (Fig. 4 a). The CQD coated glass substrate exhibited vibration peaks at ∼771.1, ∼1180.7, ∼1317.8, ∼1396.8, ∼1557.2, ∼1664.9, ∼2911.9, and ∼3180.0 cm−1, corresponding to O−C=O, C−O, C−OH, C−H, C=O, COOH, C−H, and N−H, respectively, demonstrating oxygen-containing functional groups on CQD surface. Additionally, O1s and N1s XPS spectra confirm oxygen-containing functional groups and N-doping configuration in CQDs, respectively (Fig. 4b−d). Full XPS spectrum exhibited specific peaks of C1s, N1s, O1s, and O KLL. The O KLL peak is resulted from the auger electron transition from L to K shell. The C1s, N1s, and O1s peaks are originated from the CQD. The O1s XPS consists of three peaks at ∼528.97, ∼530.94, and ∼531.97 eV, corresponding to C=O, C−OH, and C−O, respectively. This O1s XPS result demonstrates oxygen-containing functional groups on CQD, which shows consistency with C1s XPS result. The N1s XPS consists of three peaks at ∼398.95, ∼399.94, and ∼401.35 eV, corresponding to pyridinic−N, pyrrolic−N, and graphitic−N, respectively. The N-doping into CQD indicates practical pyrolysis of N-containing precursor (Urea, CO(NH2)2) during hydrothermal reaction.

To track the species produced by thermal pyrolysis of CQDs during USPD, TGA-GC/MS coupled analysis for CQD powder was investigated in the temperature range from 30 to 450 °C (Fig. 5 ). As confirmed in the TGA curve, the residual weight percentage of CQDs steadily decreases up to 450 °C, which is due to the thermal decomposition of oxygen-containing functional groups on the CQDs. Simultaneously, total ion current (TIC) was tracked by the coupled GC/MS to classify mass spectral peaks with increasing temperature. As a result, significant TIC peaks were detected at 102.77 and 112.77 °C corresponding to methane, 152.78 and 162.79 °C corresponding to ammonia, 254.93 and 264.90 °C corresponding to succinimide, 275.03, 284.90, and 295.03 °C corresponding to glutaric acid/glutarimide, respectively. These organic compounds produced by the thermal pyrolysis of CQDs are decomposed into water molecules and carbon oxides through a combustion reaction. Afterward, the resultant water molecules are transformed into oxygen molecules through a catalytic reaction at the oxygen-containing functional groups on CQDs, thereby enhancing oxygen concentration near the CQD thin film. Based on the thermal behavior of CQDs, the abundant surface oxygen-containing functional groups can be readily pyrolyzed and transformed into extra oxygen molecules, thus promoting smooth and (200) preferred FTO films. The resultant smooth morphology and preferred (200) orientation of the FTO films may have induced higher light transmissions and electrical conductivities, respectively, as compared with rough FTO films with a (110) orientation. Generally, the F-doping of SnO2 is achieved via the thermal decomposition of NH4F (NH4F → HF + NH3), which results in HF serving as an F-donor during USPD. However, the strong hydrogen bonds in HF and possible escape of HF from other pyrolysis gases restrict the available F-doping concentration in FTO films [19]. Because F-doping generates additional electrons with VO in SnO2 crystals (F:SnO2 → SnSn + OO + 1/2O2 + VO ∙∙ + FO ∙ + 3e−), the F-doping concentration in the FTO can govern the electrical conductivity of the FTO films. As compared with the bare FTO, the interfacial CQD thin film showed an increase in the F-doping concentration during USPD. The abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the CQDs induced the amicable dissociation of HF into formic acid (HCOOH+) and F− species (HF + COOH → HCOOH+ + F−) to generate fluorinated organic compounds. As can be seen in the TGA-GC/MS analysis (Fig. 5), TIC peaks related to formic acid were detected at 153.35 and 163.33 °C, while TIC peaks identifying fluorinated organic compounds containing weakly bound F− species were detected at 392.73, 402.77, 412.71, and 432.7 °C. The F− bound to the fluorinated organics can be effectively dissociated and doped into SnO2, thereby enhancing the F-doping concentration in the FTO films [20]. Additionally, the resultant formic acids can serve as reducing agent to extract oxygen atoms in SnO2 lattice, thereby generating additional VO (SnO2 + HCOOH → SnO2−x + VO + CO2 + H2O + 1/2O2) [21]. Therefore, interfacial CQDs thin films can be a unique medium for chemical bond engineering to accelerate the F-doping and increase the VO concentration of FTO films during USPD, which will enhance the carrier concentration in the FTO films, thereby improving their electrical conductivity. To optimize the effects of the interfacial CQD thin film on the morphology and chemical bonding states of the FTO films, the CQD thin film thickness was controlled at three levels and the same USPD process as that of bare FTO was adopted (named 1CQD/FTO, 2CQD/FTO, and 3CQD/FTO).

Fig. 6 a shows the XRD patterns of the bare FTO, 1CQD/FTO, 2CQD/FTO, and 3CQD/FTO for angles between 10° and 90°. All the FTO films featured a polycrystalline structure with specific diffraction peaks at approximately 26.6°, 33.9°, 38.0°, 51.8°, and 54.8°, which correspond to the (110), (101), (200), (211), and (220) planes of tetragonal rutile SnO2, respectively (JCPDS #82-0287). This polycrystalline feature confirms the crystallization of all the FTO films for achieving amicable electron conductance as a result of USPD. A close observation in the range of 24°−42° showed a gradual (200) intensity enhancement, whereas the (110) intensity reduced gradually from bare FTO to 2CQD/FTO (Fig. 6b). Accordingly, 2CQD/FTO (6.63) indicated a significant increase in the intensity ratio of the (200) plane to the (110) plane (I(200)/I(110)) unlike the bare FTO (0.93), 1CQD/FTO (2.07), and 3CQD/FTO (2.22) (Fig. 6c). The enhancement in I(200)/I(110) shown by 1CQD/FTO as compared with that of the bare FTO verified the (200) oriented crystal growth in the presence of an interfacial CQD thin film, which was derived from oxygen activation by enhanced oxygen concentration due to the thermal pyrolysis of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CQDs during USPD [16,22]. In particular, the significant increase in I(200)/I(110) shown by 2CQD/FTO signifies accelerated oxygen activation, which promotes the (200) oriented growth of FTO through the enhanced thermal pyrolysis of oxygen-containing surface functional groups owing to the increased thickness of the 2CQD thin film as compared with that of the 1CQD thin film. However, 3CQD/FTO exhibited a lower I(200)/I(110) compared with 2CQD/FTO, which may be due to the excessive CQD film thickness inducing the undesirable pyrolysis of oxygen-containing functional groups. This incomplete thermal decomposition of the CQD functional groups weakened the (200) oriented crystal growth compared with that of 2CQD/FTO. The (200) oriented growth is related to the direction parallel to the substrate, whereas the (110) orientation is related to the direction perpendicular to the substrate; the highest I(200)/I(110) achieved by 2CQD/FTO compared with those of other FTO films can simultaneously improve the optical and electrical properties, as depicted in Fig. 6d. The (200) orientation may form a dense surface morphology that can effectively facilitate electron transfer through the relaxation of electron scattering near the interface. Moreover, the resultant smooth surface can maximize transmissivity by minimizing light scattering and reflection at the film surface.

Fig. 9 a−d show the detailed chemical bonding states of all the FTO films. As shown in the Sn3d XPS result (Fig. 9a), all the FTO films exhibited two main peaks corresponding to Sn3d5/2 and Sn3d3/2 near ∼486.9 and ∼495.4 eV, respectively. Each peak can be deconvoluted into Sn-F (∼487.7 and ∼496.2 eV) and Sn-Sn (∼486.8 and ∼495.2 eV) bonds, thus indicating the substitution of oxygen with fluorine in the SnO2 to form Sn-F bonds [24]. To identify the F-doping concentration of each FTO film, we calculated the peak area ratio of Sn-F/Sn-Sn for all the FTO films (Fig. 9c). The result shows that unlike the bare FTO, 1CQD/FTO indicated an enhancement in the Sn-F/Sn-Sn ratio. This F-doping acceleration is endowed by the abundant carboxyl groups on the CQD surface, which react with HF molecules thus forming dissociated F− and formic acid. The dissociation of HF to F− can serve as a feasible F-doping source whereas the HF molecules limit effective F-doping by their strong hydrogen bonding. Based on this mechanism, one can infer that 2CQD/FTO afforded accelerated F-doping by the optimized effects of the interfacial CQD thin film generating an active HF dissociation. The F-doping concentration contributes significantly to the electrical conductivity of FTO films. When the F− anion substitutes an O2− anion in the SnO2 lattice, additional free electrons are generated near the F-doping site. The resultant additional free electrons can increase the electrical conductivity and decrease the sheet resistance of FTO films. The O1s XPS of all the FTO films featured a broad peak composed of O-Sn (∼530.6 eV), -OH (∼531.8 eV), and VO (∼532.9 eV) (Fig. 5b) [25]. Generally, VO can be created in FTO crystals during USPD process to compensate for charge imbalance and lattice distortion due to F-doping in SnO2 [26]. In addition to the F-doping effect, the VO in FTO films can provide additional free electrons to enhance electrical conductivity. Therefore, the VO concentration was identified by calculating the peak area ratio of VO/O-Sn (Fig. 9c). As a result, an acceleration of VO/O-Sn was observed from bare FTO to 2CQD/FTO. The abundant carboxyl groups on the interfacial CQD thin film reacted vigorously with HF, thus producing formic acid during USPD. The resultant formic acid functioned as strong reducing agent to generate VO in SnO2. 3CQD/FTO indicated a slight decrease in VO/O-Sn as a result of a moderated reaction for generating formic acid. The chemical structure engineering including accelerated F-doping and increased VO in 2CQD/FTO is likely to produce abundant additional free electrons that can enhance the electrical conductivity of TCEs (Fig. 9d).

To investigate the 2CQD/FTO effect on optoelectronic applications, electrochemical and EC properties of bare FTO//VO and 2CQD/FTO//VO electrodes were measured. To characterize electrochemically active VO layer, XRD and FTIR analyzes were performed on bare FTO//VO and 2CQD/FTO//VO electrodes (Fig. 12 a and b). From the XRD result, both electrodes exhibited crystalline peaks, corresponding to V2O5 (JCPDS #41-1426) and SnO2 (JCPDS #88-0287). From the FTIR result, both electrodes exhibited vibration peaks at ∼702.1, ∼865.5, and ∼1021.2 cm−1, corresponding to chemical bonds of Sn-O, V−O−V, and V=O, respectively, demonstrating consistent V2O5 phase in the electrodes. The VO film thickness of bare FTO//VO and 2CQD/FTO//VO was equally adjusted to ∼155.5−207.4 nm (Fig. 12c and d). The vacant space between bare FTO and V2O5 function as interfacial resistance for electron transfer, deteriorating electrochemical performance of electrodes. In comparison, 2CQD/FTO//VO featured a sturdy adhesion between 2CQD/FTO and V2O5, thereby providing fast and homogeneous electron transfer between the layers. Fig. 13 a shows cyclic voltammograms of bare FTO//VO and 2CQD/FTO//VO. Both electrodes revealed obvious redox behavior accompanying Li ion intercalation/deintercalation into the crystalline VO film. A pair of redox peaks near at 0.18 and −0.01 V is attributed to the two-step reduction reaction of V2O5 (V2O5 → α-Li x V2O5 → ε-Li y V2O5). A pair of oxidation peaks near at 0.19 and 0.38 V is attributed to the two-step oxidation reaction of ε-Li y V2O5 (ε-Li y V2O5 → α-Li x V2O5 → V2O5) [30]. The results of cyclic voltammograms demonstrate the potential EC functionality of bare FTO//VO and 2CQD/FTO//VO electrodes with significant electrochemical activity. Fig. 13b exhibits in-situ transmittance variations of bare FTO//VO and 2CQD/FTO//VO at the alternating potentials between −0.5 V and 1.0 V for 60 s, respectively. The bare FTO//VO and 2CQD/FTO//VO demonstrated significant transmittance modulation (transmittance difference between the bleached state and the colored state) of 36.4 % and 38.7 %, respectively, which shows an EC functionality of the fabricated electrodes (Table 2 ). Importantly, 2CQD/FTO//VO exhibited a remarkable transmittance maintenance between the 1st bleached state (41.6 %) and the 5th bleached state (41.3 %) while the bare FTO//VO showed a noticeable transmittance degradation between the 1st bleached state (42.6 %) and the 5th bleached state (37.3 %). The EC cyclic stability of 2CQD/FTO//VO may originate from the smooth and dense film morphology of 2CQD/FTO, which provides a uniform and homogeneous electron transfer between TCF (2CQD/FTO) and electrochemically active layer (VO) during redox reaction, thereby possibly reducing the interfacial resistance compared to that of bare FTO with the rough surface morphology. Fig. 13c shows zoomed-in in-situ transmittance variations of bare FTO//VO and 2CQD/FTO//VO, which displays color switching kinetics of bleaching and coloration process, respectively. As shown in the inset photographs in Fig. 13c, the fabricated EC electrodes displayed deep yellow and transparent blue in the colored and bleached state, respectively. Interestingly, 2CQD/FTO//VO exhibited faster switching speeds in both bleaching and coloration (5.3 s and 2.3 s) processes compared to bare FTO//VO (8.6 s and 4.3 s) (Table 2). The enhancement of EC switching kinetics is mainly resulted from the fast electron transfer capability of 2CQD/FTO, which is confirmed by the lower sheet resistance compared to the bare FTO. Considering that the EC mechanism generates by the redox reaction of V2O5 (colored) + xLi+ + xe− ↔ Li x V2O5 (bleached), the fast electron supply accelerates the redox reaction rate, thus adjusting the band gap for color changes. The coloration efficiency (CE), a comprehensive indicator of EC property, can be calculated by the slope of optical density with respect to charge density. As a result, 2CQD/FTO//VO exhibited a higher CE (81.1 cm2/C) compared to bare FTO//VO (69.1 cm2/C) (Fig. 13d) and existing VO coated FTO electrodes (Table 3 ) [31−33]. We therefore believe that the 2CQD/FTO with the optimized transparent conducting performances shows excellent suitability as a TCE for high-performance EC devices.

The simultaneous engineering of morphological and chemical structures on FTO films was realized using a CQD thin film as the interface layer during USPD. To demonstrate the effect of the interfacial CQD thin films on the FTO films, the thicknesses of the CQD thin films were adjusted to three levels: 1CQD/FTO, 2CQD/FTO, and 3CQD/FTO. The enhanced oxygen concentration, which was derived from the thermal decomposition of oxygen-containing functional groups on the CQDs, induced crystal growth toward the preferred (200) orientation parallel to the glass substrate, thus resulting in a smooth film morphology. The abundant carboxyl surface functional groups on the CQDs induced the dissociation of HF into formic acid and F−, and the resultant F− was effectively doped into SnO2, thereby increasing the F-doping concentration. Additionally, the resultant formic acids might have served as reducing agents to extract oxygen atoms from the SnO2 lattice, thereby increasing in VO concentration. The accelerated F-doping and increase in VO concentration generated abundant free electrons, thus increasing the carrier concentration. Among the FTO films, 2CQD/FTO showed superior transparent conducting performances, including a sheet resistance of ∼5.7 Ω/□, a visible transmittance of ∼85.9 %, and a figure of merit of 3.84 × 10−2 Ω−1 as compared with the bare FTO, 1CQD/FTO, and 3CQD/FTO. Accordingly, 2CQD/FTO used as TCE in EC electrodes demonstrated improved EC performance, including faster switching speeds (5.3 s and 2.3 s for bleaching and coloration, respectively) and higher coloration efficiency (81.1 cm2/C) compared to the bare FTO.

Ki-Won Lee: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Myeong-Hun Jo: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Hyo-Jin Ahn: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Nano & Material Technology Development Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by Ministry of Science and ICT (No. 2021M3H4A3A02086102) and the Nano & Material Technology Development Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by Ministry of Science and ICT (No. 2021M3D1A2047047).
